title: Team Topologies & Conway's Law for Staff+ Engineers
description: How Staff+ engineers use Team Topologies (Team Topologies by Skelton & Pais) and Conway's Law to design organizations that produce better software. Covers the four fundamental team types (stream-aligned, platform, enabling, complicated-subsystem), the three interaction modes (collaboration, X-as-a-Service, facilitating), reverse Conway maneuvers, cognitive load limits, and how SAP's traditional component teams map to modern topology thinking.

description.value:
Conway's Law (Melvin Conway, 1968): 'Organizations which design systems are constrained to produce designs which are copies of the communication structures of these organizations.' In plain English: the architecture of your software will mirror the structure of your organization. If your teams are split as 'frontend team,' 'backend team,' 'database team,' your architecture will have a frontend service, a backend service, and a database — regardless of whether that's the best architecture.

Reverse Conway maneuver: Instead of accepting the organizational structure and building software that mirrors it, you deliberately restructure your teams to produce the architecture you want. Want microservices with clean bounded contexts? Organize your teams around business capabilities (stream-aligned teams) and give each team ownership of its services. The 'reverse Conway' is one of the most powerful tools a Staff+ engineer has for driving architectural change. Example: Amazon's 'two-pizza teams' and 'API as product' culture produced Amazon's service-oriented architecture. Netflix's 'team per microservice' produced Netflix's cloud-native architecture.

SAP Bridge: SAP's traditional development organization is organized by module (FI, CO, SD, MM, PP, etc.) — which Conway's Law explains perfectly: the software is organized into silos that mirror the organizational silos. When SAP moved to S/4HANA and clean core, they attempted a reverse Conway — reorganizing teams around end-to-end business processes (Lead-to-Cash, Source-to-Pay, Design-to-Operate) rather than modules. This is exactly the reverse Conway maneuver. SAP engineers who have lived through this reorganization understand the concept intuitively.

Stream-aligned team: The default team type. A stream-aligned team is aligned to a single, valuable stream of work — a business domain, a product capability, a user journey. The team owns the full lifecycle of its stream: feature development, operations, quality, security. Key properties: (1) The team is responsible for end-to-end outcomes, not piecemeal outputs. (2) The team can make changes to its stream independently (no waiting for other teams). (3) The team size follows the 'cognitive load' limit — the team cannot be responsible for more than it can keep in its collective head. (4) Amazon's 'two-pizza team' (6-10 people) is a stream-aligned team. At FAANG, stream-aligned teams own services like 'Checkout,' 'Search,' 'Recommendations' — each is a full business capability.

Platform team: A platform team enables stream-aligned teams to deliver faster by providing internal services, tools, and infrastructure. The platform is a 'product' that stream-aligned teams consume. Key properties: (1) The platform team treats stream-aligned teams as their customers — they must provide good developer experience, documentation, SLAs. (2) The platform must reduce the cognitive load on stream-aligned teams — if using the platform is harder than building their own solution, the platform is failing. (3) Examples: Kubernetes platform, CI/CD pipeline, feature flag service, internal developer portal (Backstage). (4) At FAANG, platform teams include the 'Infrastructure Platform' (Kubernetes, networking), 'Data Platform' (data warehouse, feature store), and 'Developer Platform' (CI/CD, testing).

Enabling team: An enabling team has deep expertise in a specific technical domain and helps stream-aligned teams build capability. They are temporary — they work themselves out of a job. Key properties: (1) They answer questions, teach, and pair with stream-aligned teams. (2) They do NOT build features — they build capability. (3) Example: a 'Performance Optimization Enabling Team' helps stream-aligned teams improve their service's latency and throughput. (4) At FAANG, enabling teams include the 'SRE Enablement Team' (helping service teams implement SLOs and error budgets).

Complicated-subsystem team: Some technical domains are so complex that a dedicated team is needed. Key properties: (1) The domain requires deep specialist knowledge. (2) Other teams cannot be expected to develop this expertise. (3) The team provides a simple interface to the complicated subsystem. (4) Examples: Video encoding engine, payment reconciliation engine, ML training infrastructure, 3D rendering engine. (5) At FAANG, complicated-subsystem teams include 'Video Transcoding' at YouTube, 'GraphQL Federation Engine' at Meta, 'Product Search Index' at Amazon.

Cognitive load as the limiting factor: A core insight of Team Topologies is that teams have a finite cognitive capacity. A team can only hold a certain amount of information in its collective working memory. When a team's cognitive load exceeds its capacity, velocity drops, quality degrades, and burnout increases. The three types of cognitive load: (1) Intrinsic — the complexity inherent in the work the team does. (2) Extraneous — the overhead of processes, tools, and context-switching (minimize this). (3) Germane — the learning the team needs to do to become more effective (invest in this). The Staff+ engineer's job is to keep each team's intrinsic cognitive load within its capacity — by splitting the system into independently understandable parts and providing platforms that reduce extraneous load.

Three interaction modes between teams: (1) Collaboration — two teams work together for a limited period on a shared goal. Used when: a new service crosses team boundaries, a platform team and stream-aligned team need to define the API together, or a novel problem requires combined expertise. Collaboration is expensive (two teams' time) and should be time-boxed. (2) X-as-a-Service — one team provides a service to another team with a well-defined API. The consuming team treats the providing team as a vendor. Used when: the interface is stable, the consuming team can be self-sufficient, and the cognitive load of the providing team's domain is properly encapsulated. (3) Facilitating — one team helps another team learn a new capability. The facilitating team transfers skills, not code. Used when: the enabling team is helping a stream-aligned team adopt a new technology or practice.

How Staff+ engineers use Team Topologies at FAANG: (1) When joining a new organization, map the current team topology — document team types, interaction modes, and cognitive load distribution. (2) Identify mismatches — a team doing both platform AND complicated-subsystem work probably has too much cognitive load. Teams collaborating for months should move to X-as-a-Service. (3) Propose a topology change (this is a Staff+ level initiative — writing a 'Team Topologies proposal' that suggests re-organizing teams to reduce cognitive load and improve flow).

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Team Topologies knowledge is increasingly expected at Staff+ interviews, especially for Tech Lead and Platform roles. Key signals: (1) Understanding Conway's Law and the Reverse Conway maneuver — shows organizational design thinking. (2) The four team types and when to use each — shows you know how to structure engineering organizations. (3) Cognitive load as a constraint — shows Staff+ level systems thinking. (4) Suggesting team topology changes in behavioral answers — 'I noticed our platform team was overwhelmed, so I proposed splitting it into two teams: one focused on the internal API, one focused on infrastructure' — demonstrates leadership and organizational impact.
